title: Q3SAT-GPT: A Generative Model for Discovering Quantum Circuits for the 3-SAT Problem
authors: Pratim Ugale, Ilya Tyagin, Karunya Shirali, Kien X. Nguyen, Ilya Safro
abstract: This work introduces Q3SAT-GPT, a generative model for discovering quantum circuits for the Max-E3-SAT problem. Our method learns from high-performing QAOA-style ansätze to directly generate candidate circuits. To create high-quality supervision, we also introduce Mosaic Adaptive QAOA (MosaicADAPT-QAOA), an adaptive strategy for constructing low-depth QAOA circuits by selecting subsets of mixer operators in each step, rather than inserting operators sequentially. The resulting circuits serve as training data for the generative model, allowing it to learn effective circuit design patterns while eliminating the need for costly variational optimization at inference time. Experiments show that our framework attains strong solution quality with shallow circuits and scales significantly better than both our adaptive construction procedure and conventional variational baselines. Our results establish generative modeling as a high-performance route toward the scalable discovery of quantum optimization circuits, demonstrating that these models can effectively internalize circuit logic while providing a foundation for future, instance-aware inductive biases. Reproducibility: The source code is available at https://github.com/pratimugale/Q3SAT-GPT.
summary: This paper introduces Q3SAT-GPT, a generative AI model that learns to design quantum circuits for solving 3-SAT problems. By training on circuits produced by a new adaptive algorithm (MosaicADAPT-QAOA), the model can bypass expensive optimization steps and propose high-quality solutions in a single step. This approach offers a scalable route toward discovering efficient quantum algorithms for combinatorial optimization.
main_problem: The challenge of efficiently discovering high-quality, low-depth quantum circuits for the Max-E3-SAT combinatorial optimization problem without the heavy computational cost of iterative variational optimization.
main_result: The Q3SAT-GPT generative model can predict optimized quantum circuits in a single forward pass with high approximation ratios and significantly faster amortized inference time compared to classical optimization-based adaptive methods.
method: A generative transformer-based architecture (Q3SAT-GPT) is trained using data generated by a new adaptive strategy called MosaicADAPT-QAOA, which uses a Maximum Weight Independent Set approach to select multiple disjoint operators per layer.
model_or_system: The problem involves Max-E3-SAT instances (Uniform Random and Balanced) mapped to a cost Hamiltonian using a Higher-Order Unconstrained Binary Optimization (HUBO) formulation with Pauli-Z operators.
key_observables: Approximation Ratio (AR), Circuit Error Rate (ER), and circuit depth.
important_parameters: Phase transition ratio (alpha), number of variables (n), and the initial gamma parameter (gamma_0).
main_assumptions: The performance of the generative model is bounded by the quality of the training data produced by the MosaicADTP-QAOA algorithm; the focus is limited to the Max-E3-SAT problem class.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the conflict in greedy operator selection; Figure 2 illustrates an incompatibility graph; Figure 3 details the model pipeline; Figure 4 compares energy convergence; Figure 5 shows gradient norms; Figure 6 shows operator evolution; Figure 7 compares predicted vs. optimized parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of circuit discovery, presents the MosaicADAPT-QAOA adaptive strategy, describes the Q3SAT-GPT generative architecture and training pipeline, evaluates performance against classical and quantum baselines, and discusses limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: A Representative Conflict Illustration. Operator gener- ators ˆAi are labeled with their corresponding gradient mag- nitudes in parentheses. In (a), selecting the highest individual gradient ( ˆA1) blocks the selection possibility of ˆA4 and ˆA5, leading to a lower total sum of gradients as compared to (b).

Fig. 2: Incompatibility graph representation for a 3-qubit problem instance, used for operator selection. Each node in the graph represents a candidate unitary operator from the pool P, An edge connects two nodes if and only if their corresponding operators have overlapping qubit support. Sets of overlapping two-qubit operators form fully connected cliques and are omitted to maintain legibility.
Fig. 3: Detailed pipeline demonstrating formatting, tokenization, and embeddings. The | delimiter is used to segment clauses, with explicit OR (∨) tokens omitted.

Fig. 4: Comparison of the energy convergence across adap- tations. Note that in Figure (b), plots of TETRIS-QAOA and ADAPT-QAOA overlap due to the same operators being chosen by both the methods (see Figure 6).

Fig. 5: Comparison of the maximum gradient norm across all operators in the mixer pool. Note that in Figure (b), plots of TETRIS-QAOA and ADAPT-QAOA overlap due to the same operators having the maximum gradient for the most part (see Figure 6).
Fig. 6: Evolution of the operator selection across adaptations. Note that the count inside a bar is that of the total number of operators selected in that layer across all instances.

Fig. 7: Comparison of Q3SAT-GPT predicted parameters against MosaicADAPT-QAOA optimized parameters on the test set. Solid lines denote the mean parameter value across samples, and shaded regions denote ±1 standard deviation. This plot is for the configuration containing 12 qubits, with γ0 = 0.5, and utilizes FEATHER embeddings. Since there are multiple beta values at a particular layer for MosaicADAPT- QAOA, all beta values are averaged at that layer.
